title: Decision Trees in Practice
concept: Applying decision trees to make predictions involves starting at the root, answering the sequential questions about an instance's features, and following the path to a leaf node.

options.text: The reduction in entropy or Gini impurity achieved by splitting data.
The weight of the leaf node in the final decision.
The learning rate scale factor of a tree.

exercises.explain: Correct! Information gain measures how much cleaner or less messy the data groups become after a split.

cards.title: Will we play tennis today?
cards.body: Imagine deciding whether to play tennis. You look outside: is it sunny, overcast, or rainy? If it's sunny, is it humid? If it's rainy, is it windy? Instead of guessing, you follow a set of historical guidelines. This classic example shows how we trace a trained tree to make real-world decisions.

cards.title: Tracing the path
cards.body: To predict with a decision tree, you take a new data point and start at the **root node**. You read the question, check the point's feature value, follow the corresponding branch, and repeat the process at the next node until you hit a **leaf node**.

cards.title: A medical diagnostic flow
cards.body: When you visit a clinic, the nurse checks: 1. Do you have a fever? Yes -> 2. Is your throat sore? Yes -> Diagnose: Strep throat. This flow of checkups is a decision tree path. A patient (instance) enters, checks symptoms (features), and gets a diagnosis (prediction).

cards.title: Predicting car loan approval
cards.body: An applicant has Income = $60k, Credit Score = 650. The model splits: 1. Is Income > $50k? Yes. Branch routes to: 2. Is Credit Score > 700? No. Branch routes to: Leaf: 'Reject'. Even though their income is high, their credit score routes them to a reject leaf.

cards.title: Trees can handle numbers too
cards.body: You might assume decision trees only work for categorical features like 'Sunny' or 'Rainy'. In reality, trees handle numerical features by finding the best boundary split (e.g. 'Income > $45,000'). It sorts the numbers, tests splits between values, and picks the split that yields the highest information gain.

cards.title: Tracing a Tennis Decision
cards.body: Trace an outlook path to predict play:

left.text:
Outlook: Sunny
Check Humidity: High?
Yes -> Leaf: Don't Play
No -> Leaf: Play

right.text:
Outlook: Overcast
No further checks needed.
Leaf: Play (100% historical play rate)

exercises.prompt: You trace a decision tree for a loan applicant. The root checks: 'Debt-to-Income < 40%'. The applicant has 35%. What is the next step?

options.text: Follow the 'Yes' branch to the next internal node or leaf node.
Follow the 'No' branch and immediately reject the applicant.
Stop the model and recompute the Gini impurity.
Calculate the average debt of all other applicants.

exercises.explain: Correct! Since 35% is less than 40%, the check is True/Yes, so you follow the Yes branch.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Decision trees can only split on text categories (like 'Red' or 'Blue') and cannot split on numerical features (like 'Weight > 10 lbs').
exercises.explain: False! Decision trees handle numerical columns by creating boundary thresholds (e.g. feature > value) to split data.

exercises.sentence: To predict with a decision tree, you take an instance and trace it from the ___ to a leaf.
exercises.explain: Every prediction trace starts at the root node at the top of the tree.

exercises.prompt: A tree decides if a user will click an ad: 1. Is age < 30? Yes -> 2. Is device = Mobile? No -> Predict: 'No Click'. For a user aged 25 on a desktop computer, what is the tree's prediction?

options.text: 'No Click', because age is under 30 (Yes) and device is not Mobile (No)
'Click', because they are on a desktop

exercises.explain: Correct! Tracing the path: Age 25 is < 30 (Yes) -> Device is Desktop, so 'device = Mobile' is No -> Routes to 'No Click'.

exercises.prompt: A decision tree classifies fruits based on features: 1. Is weight > 100g? Yes -> 2. Is color yellow? Yes -> 'Banana', No -> 'Apple'. No -> 3. Is color red? Yes -> 'Cherry', No -> 'Grape'. What is the prediction for a fruit that weighs 80g and is red?

options.text: 'Cherry', because weight is not > 100g (No) -> routes to check 3 -> color is red (Yes) -> 'Cherry'
'Apple', because it matches the weight path

exercises.explain: Exactly! The fruit weighs 80g, so 'weight > 100g' is No. This routes the fruit to the right side of the tree to check 3. Since its color is red, it matches the Yes branch, resulting in the prediction 'Cherry'. Congratulations on completing the Decision Trees unit! Next, let's learn why these trees can overfit and how to control them!
